subject.description: White male, mid-30s, slightly tired eyes, plain white button-down shirt (top button open, no tie). Face caught in a very specific expression: both hands are flat on the desk, he's leaned forward slightly, looking straight at camera — not at a screen. Expression is the face of someone who has just accepted an absurd reality. Micro-expression: eyebrows slightly raised, the corners of the mouth pulled very slightly down — not frowning, just the face of 'this is actually happening.' Zero performance. Real skin texture, slight redness at cheeks, no retouching.
subject.action: Hands flat on desk, leaned forward toward camera. Still. Not typing, not working. Just... stopped. The stillness of someone who knows they're about to have a very difficult conversation and is mentally preparing.

setting.location: Small open-plan office or agency space — other desks blurred in the background, one other person's silhouette barely visible behind him. He's the only one who stopped.
setting.time_of_day: Mid-morning — cool overhead office lighting, a window with flat daylight behind him.

composition.camera_angle: Eye-level, dead center. He's looking directly into the lens.
composition.framing: Medium close-up — head and shoulders, hands barely visible at bottom of frame resting on desk. Full head in frame, natural headspace. Chin fully visible with breathing room.

camera_settings.depth_of_field: shallow — background office blurred, subject sharp

lighting.type: Flat overhead office fluorescent with cool daylight from window behind. Slightly unflattering — real office light, not optimized.
lighting.color_grading: Cool neutral, slightly desaturated. No warmth added. Feels like the day got colder when the news hit.

mood: Pre-conversation stillness. The specific quiet of someone who has fully absorbed bad news and is now deciding what to do with it. Not reactive — post-reactive. The calm before the hard conversation.
aesthetic_style: 8K ultra realistic, hyper realistic photorealism. High-end editorial documentary photography — the kind shot for a business magazine feature on modern work culture. Real skin, real light, no production polish.
